clean_story:
Undead hordes. Mirrors of human fear.

Zombie comes from Haitian folklore’s zonbi—a corpse reanimated by magic, not disease. Early films stole this idea but flipped it: no voodoo priests, just plagues and science gone wrong.

The first zombie film, White Zombie (1932), treated them like hypnotized slaves. Then Night of the Living Dead (1968) rewrote the rules: zombies as slow, relentless eaters of flesh, spreading like a virus. No explanation. No cure. Just survival. This wasn’t horror—it was a pressure cooker for real-world anxieties. Cold War nuclear dread? Zombies as radiation victims. Consumerism? Mindless shoppers in Dawn of the Dead (1978). Pandemics? 28 Days Later (2002) made them sprint.

The mechanics are simple: bite = infection. Speed varies (shamblers vs. sprinters), but the core stays—humanity collapses under sheer numbers. Films use gore as distraction; the real terror is how quickly society unravels. Train to Busan (2016) traps survivors on a bullet train, turning strangers into allies or threats in minutes. The Girl with All the Gifts (2016) twists the trope—what if the "cure" erases humanity?

Zombies endure because they’re blank slates. They don’t need motives. They’re the storm you can’t outrun.
Three related subjects are viral outbreak films, post-apocalyptic survival, and folklore’s undead variants.
